exercises.title: AtomicInteger basics — always correct
hints: AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet() is a single atomic CPU operation
No race condition — always exactly 2000
Compare: regular int++ would give wrong result every time

exercises.title: ConcurrentHashMap atomic merge
exercises.scenario: merge() is the correct atomic increment on ConcurrentHashMap.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blank to atomically increment request counts.

blanks.hint: Atomic: if absent put value; else apply remapping function

hints: merge(key, value, fn): if absent → put value; if present → fn(existing, value)
merge('orders', 1, Integer::sum): 0+1=1, then 1+1=2

exercises.title: Race condition vs AtomicInteger
exercises.scenario: Show that int++ races; AtomicInteger doesn't.
exercises.instructions:
Run two versions: (1) regular int incremented by 2 threads 5000 times each, (2) AtomicInteger same setup. Print both results.

Expected output:
int result: [some value < 10000] (varies)
Atomic result: 10000
exercises.starter_code: // Race condition demo: int vs AtomicInteger
hints: rawCount++ is three operations: READ, ADD, WRITE — subject to race
atomicCount.incrementAndGet() is one atomic CPU instruction
The int result will almost certainly be < 10000

exercises.title: ConcurrentHashMap — thread-safe frequency count
exercises.scenario: Count word frequencies across multiple threads.
exercises.instructions:
Use ConcurrentHashMap.merge() to count word frequencies from 4 threads processing different word lists. Print counts sorted alphabetically.

Words across threads: 'apple','apple','banana','cherry','banana','apple','cherry','cherry'

Expected output:
apple: 3
banana: 2
cherry: 3
hints: merge(word, 1, Integer::sum) atomically increments
TreeMap for sorted output
4 batches submitted to pool — all run concurrently

exercises.title: computeIfAbsent — safe lazy initialization
exercises.scenario: computeIfAbsent atomically creates value if key is absent.
exercises.instructions:
Use computeIfAbsent to group employees by department (thread-safe). 4 threads add employees concurrently.

Expected output (sorted):
Engineering: [Aisha, Priya]
Marketing: [Vikram]
Sales: [Raj, Anita]
hints: computeIfAbsent(dept, k -> new CopyOnWriteArrayList<>()): atomically creates list if absent
CopyOnWriteArrayList because multiple threads may add to same department list
TreeMap for sorted output

exercises.title: BlockingQueue producer-consumer
exercises.scenario: Bounded queue with producer blocking when full.
exercises.instructions:
Create a producer that generates 5 tasks ('Task-1' through 'Task-5') and a consumer that processes them (prints 'Processing: Task-N'). Use LinkedBlockingQueue(3).

Expected output (in order):
Producing: Task-1
Producing: Task-2
Producing: Task-3
Processing: Task-1
...(all 5 tasks produced and consumed)
hints: queue.put() blocks if queue is full (capacity=3)
queue.take() blocks if queue is empty
Sentinel 'DONE' signals consumer to stop

exercises.title: AtomicLong for high-throughput counter
exercises.scenario: AtomicLong handles high-contention counting without locks.
exercises.instructions:
Simulate a web server with 10 threads each handling 10,000 requests. Count total with AtomicLong. Print final count and requests per thread.

Expected output:
Total requests: 100000
Per thread: 10000
hints: AtomicLong.incrementAndGet() — atomic, no synchronization needed
10 threads × 10000 = 100000 — exact, no lost increments
Correct despite 10 concurrent threads

exercises.title: ConcurrentHashMap — cache with computeIfAbsent
exercises.scenario: Thread-safe lazy cache for expensive computations.
exercises.instructions:
Build a simple cache using ConcurrentHashMap. The 'expensive' computation takes 200ms (sleep). Show that concurrent requests for the same key all get the same result but computation runs only once.

Expected output (approx):
Computing for: 'hyderabad'
Result for 'hyderabad': HYDERABAD
Result for 'hyderabad': HYDERABAD  [from cache — no 'Computing' printed again]
Total computations: 1
hints: computeIfAbsent: only calls the function if key is absent
AtomicInteger tracks how many times computation ran
Multiple threads may race — computeIfAbsent handles this safely

exercises.title: AtomicBoolean — one-time initialization
exercises.scenario: compareAndSet ensures only one thread performs initialization.
exercises.instructions:
10 threads all try to be the first to 'initialize the system'. Only one should succeed. Use AtomicBoolean.compareAndSet.

Expected output:
Thread-N initialized the system!
[9 lines:] Thread-M: system already initialized
hints: compareAndSet(false, true): only first thread gets true back
Exactly ONE thread will see wasFirst=true
All 9 others see false — system already initialized

exercises.title: CopyOnWriteArrayList for event listeners
exercises.instructions:
A notification system where 3 publishers send events and 1 thread adds/removes listeners concurrently. Show no ConcurrentModificationException.

Expected output (approx):
Listeners at start: 3
Event from publisher: ['notified L1', 'notified L2', 'notified L3']
Listeners after modify: 2
hints: Iterator over CopyOnWriteArrayList sees a snapshot — no CME
remove() creates a new copy of the array
Publisher's iterator continues on old snapshot even after remove

exercises.title: BlockingQueue — bounded work queue with multiple workers
exercises.scenario: Multiple producers feeding multiple consumers via bounded queue.
exercises.instructions:
2 producers generate 10 tasks each (20 total). 3 consumers process them. Use LinkedBlockingQueue(5). Print when each task is processed.

Expected output (order varies):
Produced Task-P1-1
...
Worker-N processed Task-P1-1
...
All 20 tasks processed
hints: POISON pill pattern: one sentinel consumed and re-queued for all workers
queue.put() blocks producers when queue is full (capacity=5)
AtomicInteger for thread-safe task count

exercises.title: ConcurrentHashMap.merge semantics
exercises.scenario: Trace merge: first call puts, subsequent calls apply function.
hints: merge: if absent → put value; if present → fn(existing, value)

exercises.title: Thread-safe LRU cache using ConcurrentHashMap
exercises.scenario: Cache with capacity limit — concurrent access safe.
exercises.instructions:
Build a simple thread-safe cache using ConcurrentHashMap that:
- Stores up to 3 entries
- Evicts the oldest entry when full (track insertion order)
- put(key, value), get(key) → Optional<String>
- Multiple threads put/get concurrently

Expected output:
get('a'): Optional[1]
get('d'): Optional[4]
get('a'): Optional.empty  (evicted when 'd' added)

test_cases.expected_stdout:
get('a'): Optional[1]
get('d'): Optional[4]
get('a'): Optional.empty

hints: Track insertion order in a LinkedBlockingDeque
When at capacity, pollFirst() removes oldest key, remove from map
synchronized on put() coordinates the two data structures

exercises.title: Analytics dashboard — all concurrent types
exercises.instructions:
Build a server analytics system:
- Requests arrive via BlockingQueue
- Worker threads consume and count: ConcurrentHashMap<endpoint, AtomicLong> for counts
- After 20 requests: print top 3 endpoints by request count

Expected output (counts vary by order):
[processing 20 requests...]
Top 3 endpoints:
/api/orders: N
/api/users: M
/api/search: P
hints: BlockingQueue.poll(500ms) returns null after timeout — used as shutdown signal
computeIfAbsent + AtomicLong for thread-safe per-endpoint counters
Sort entrySet by value descending, limit 3

exercises.title: Connection pool — BlockingQueue + AtomicInteger
exercises.scenario: A database connection pool using BlockingQueue for connection management.
exercises.instructions:
Build a `ConnectionPool` with capacity 3:
- `getConnection()` — blocks until a connection is available
- `releaseConnection(conn)` — returns connection to pool
- `getActiveCount()` — connections currently in use (AtomicInteger)
- 5 threads each borrow a connection, use it (100ms), release it

Expected output (order varies):
Thread-N acquired connection C-M
...
Thread-N released connection C-M
...
All done. Active: 0, Pool: 3
hints: BlockingQueue.take() blocks when pool is empty — natural wait for connections
AtomicInteger tracks currently-borrowed connections
5 threads, 3 connections: 3 run immediately, 2 wait for a release
